messages.content: Build a strategy: buy when price closes above its 20-day high and RSI(14) is over 55, exit on a 10-day low. Test top 40, 8% stop, from 2022.

I'll build this as a custom equity strategy — a Donchian breakout (20-day high) filtered by RSI momentum, with a 10-day low exit.Here are the real results.

**Setup used (stated for full disclosure):**
- Entry: close > prior 20-day high **and** RSI(14) > 55 (Wilder's RSI)
- Exit: close < prior 10-day low, **or** 8% stop-loss
- Universe: top 40 stocks · Capital: ₹10,00,000 · Period: 2022-01-01 → 2026-06-30
- Defaults I didn't specify (max_positions, cost_pct, trail, target) left at engine defaults — no per-trade cost or position cap was set, so treat returns as slightly optimistic.

**Results:**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 752 |
| Win rate | 38.2% |
| Total return | +76.2% |
| CAGR | 13.5% |
| Max drawdown | −23.6% (−₹2,36,000) |

**Honest read:**
- **Classic trend-follower profile:** low win rate (38%) but profitable overall — meaning the winners run much bigger than the losers. That's expected for breakout systems, but it's psychologically hard to trade and relies on a few big winners.
- **Return/drawdown ~3.2x** (76% return vs 24% DD) over 4.5 years is respectable, but the 13.5% CAGR isn't dramatically better than a simple index — worth benchmarking against buy-and-hold.
- **752 trades is a healthy sample**, so the edge isn't just a fluke of a couple of names.
- **Caveat:** no transaction costs were applied. With 752 trades, even ~0.1% round-trip cost matters. I'd re-run with `cost_pct` set realistically before trusting the number.

Want me to (a) add realistic costs + a position cap, or (b) sweep the RSI filter (e.g. 50/55/60) and breakout lookback to see how sensitive the edge is? I'd suggest just one small sweep to check robustness rather than chasing the best number.
Write a Bollinger Band mean-reversion: buy when close is below the lower band (20, 2σ), exit at the middle band. Top 30, from 2021.

I'll build this Bollinger Band mean-reversion strategy: buy when close pierces below the lower band (20-day SMA, 2σ), exit when it reverts back to the middle band (the 20-day SMA).Here are the real results.

**Setup used (stated for disclosure):**
- Entry: close < lower band (20-day SMA − 2σ)
- Exit: close ≥ middle band (20-day SMA)
- Universe: top 30 · Capital: ₹10,00,000 · Period: 2021-01-01 → 2026-06-30
- No stop-loss, no target%, no max_hold, no position cap, no costs specified — all left at engine defaults. **You didn't ask for a stop, so there's none** — that matters (see below).

**Results:**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 615 |
| Win rate | 68.3% |
| Total return | +34.4% |
| CAGR | 5.55% |
| Max drawdown | −25.8% (−₹2,57,700) |

**Honest read:**
- **Textbook mean-reversion profile:** high win rate (68%) but modest total return — the opposite of your breakout system. The exit at the mean caps winners while losers can run, so a few bad trades do the damage.
- **Weak return-to-risk:** +34% return against −26% drawdown over 5.5 years, and a 5.5% CAGR that likely *underperforms* buy-and-hold on the same names. The high win rate is seductive but the math isn't compelling here.
- **No stop = fat left tail.** With no stop-loss, a stock that keeps falling below the band stays open until it reverts (or never does). That's the classic "picking up pennies" risk. Adding an 8% stop would cut some winners short but cap the tail — worth testing the tradeoff.
- **615 trades** — good sample, so the mediocre edge is real, not noise.

Want me to re-run with an 8% stop-loss and a max-hold (say 15 days) to see if capping the tail improves the risk-adjusted return? That's the one change most likely to matter here.
Create a momentum strategy: buy the top stocks up more than 15% over 60 days, exit if they drop 8% from the peak.

I'll build this as a cross-sectional momentum strategy: enter when a stock's 60-day return exceeds 15%, and exit via an 8% trailing stop from the peak (a "give back 8% from the high" chandelier-style exit).Here are the real results.

**Setup used (stated for disclosure):**
- Entry: 60-day return > 15% (buy strength)
- Exit: **8% trailing stop from the peak** — the engine's `trail_stop_pct` handles the "drop 8% from peak" logic, so the exit rides the trend and only sells after an 8% giveback
- Universe: top 40 · Capital: ₹10,00,000 · Period: 2022-01-01 → 2026-06-30
- No hard stop-loss, target, max_hold, position cap, or costs specified — engine defaults.

⚠️ **One thing I changed from your spec:** you said "top 40" only in the previous strategies — here you just said "the top stocks." I used **top 40** for consistency with your breakout run. Say the word if you want top 30 or another size.

**Results:**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 681 |
| Win rate | 44.1% |
| Total return | +71.7% |
| CAGR | 12.85% |
| Max drawdown | −26.3% (−₹2,63,400) |

**Honest read:**
- **Trend-following profile again:** 44% win rate with a positive total return — winners run (the trail lets them), losers get cut. Similar shape to your Donchian breakout (76%/24% DD), and this one is a touch worse on risk-adjusted terms: 72% return against a *bigger* 26% drawdown.
- **The 15% threshold buys stocks that already moved a lot** — classic momentum, but you're entering late, so some entries are near local tops and get stopped out quickly (drags the win rate).
- **No costs applied** and 681 trades — a real cost assumption (~0.1%+ round trip) would meaningfully dent that 12.85% CAGR. Treat it as an upper bound.
- **Overlap caution:** all three of your trend systems (Donchian, this, MA-crossover) are essentially the *same bet* on momentum. They'll win and lose together — not diversification.

Want me to sweep the momentum threshold (10 / 15 / 20%) and/or the trail (6 / 8 / 12%) to check how sensitive the edge is — just one small grid to test robustness, not to cherry-pick the peak?
